screen_donor_acceptor_layout.purpose: Summarize donor-acceptor segmentation and conjugation-level layout from bounded structural evidence.

screen_rotor_torsion_topology.purpose: Summarize rotor density, torsion candidates, and branching topology from bounded structural evidence.

screen_planarity_compactness.purpose: Summarize planarity, compactness, principal span, and conformer-dispersion proxies.

screen_intramolecular_hbond_preorganizat.purpose: Inspect reusable prepared geometry for intramolecular H-bond and typed donor/acceptor preorganization cues.
screen_intramolecular_hbond_preorganizat.unsupported_requests_note: Without shared prepared geometry, only motif-level fallback screening is available.

screen_conformer_geometry_proxy.purpose: Summarize available conformer-level geometry proxies and single-conformer limitations.

screen_neutral_aromatic_structure.purpose: Summarize aromatic-core dominance, ring-system rigidity, and neutral-aromatic structural cues.

state_snapshot.user_query: Assess the likely AIE mechanism for this molecule.

hypothesis_pool.name: neutral aromatic
hypothesis_pool.rationale: The verifier supplement is sufficient and high-information for the neutral aromatic versus TICT pair, and it aligns with existing internal evidence: baseline screening, direct torsion screening, and donor-acceptor layout screening all favor a locally excited neutral aromatic interpretation over a twist-enabled charge-separated one in this molecule.

hypothesis_pool.name: TICT
hypothesis_pool.rationale: TICT remains the main runner-up only because it was the last meaningful challenger, but the pairwise verifier support, direct torsion screening, and absent donor-acceptor segmentation all weaken it further.

hypothesis_pool.name: ICT
hypothesis_pool.rationale: ICT stays disfavored because the structure-level donor-acceptor layout screen found no overlooked donor-acceptor partitioning, and prior CT-directed internal screening was capability-blocked rather than supportive.

hypothesis_pool.name: unknown
hypothesis_pool.rationale: Unknown remains only as residual model uncertainty; the residual unknown debt was directly screened and reduced by the negative donor-acceptor layout result.

hypothesis_pool.name: ESIPT
hypothesis_pool.rationale: ESIPT remains effectively excluded within this run because no proton-transfer-capable motif was present and it was already dropped with reason.

hypothesis_screening_ledger.screening_note: A direct ICT-oriented microscopic parse remained capability-blocked, but the donor-acceptor layout screen directly found no overlooked donor-acceptor segmentation in this all-carbon scaffold, so ICT is weakened on molecule-specific structural grounds.

A bounded molecule-specific torsion-snapshot run directly probed a central aryl-vinyl dihedral and did not produce a positive TICT-rescuing signal. The new targeted verifier supplement for the neutral aromatic versus TICT pair also supports contraction away from TICT at close-family level.

The residual unknown bucket was directly screened by bounded donor-acceptor layout analysis targeted at overlooked segmentation or charge-separation-enabling structure, and that screen returned negative.
No proton-transfer-capable motif was present in the screened structure, so ESIPT was not a realistic leading mechanism in this run.

Macro structural screening, bounded microscopic baseline, direct torsion screening, donor-acceptor layout screening, and the targeted verifier supplement all align with a neutral aromatic interpretation for this highly aromatic all-carbon scaffold.

hypothesis_reweight_history.ICT: ICT stays credible but reduced because the structure lacks classical donor-acceptor partitioning and heteroatom-based push-pull motifs.
hypothesis_reweight_history.TICT: TICT rises relative to ESIPT because several phenyl rotors and torsion candidates create a plausible twisting channel even in a hydrocarbon scaffold.
hypothesis_reweight_history.ESIPT: ESIPT falls to very low weight because there are no hetero atoms or proton-transfer-capable functional groups.
hypothesis_reweight_history.neutral aromatic: Neutral aromatic rises to the lead because the molecule is dominated by extended fused and pendant aromatic carbons with no obvious polar mechanism trigger.
hypothesis_reweight_history.unknown: Unknown remains nonzero because structural priors alone are not decisive and first-round evidence has not yet been collected.

hypothesis_reweight_history.neutral aromatic: Raised because both macro structure and bounded S0/S1 evidence are consistent with a neutral aromatic hydrocarbon framework and no alternative has gained direct support.
hypothesis_reweight_history.ICT: Lowered because donor-acceptor segmentation remains absent and the new evidence does not positively indicate classical CT, though direct CT screening is still pending.
hypothesis_reweight_history.TICT: Slightly raised relative to ICT as the main challenger because multiple phenyl rotors keep twist-mediated behavior credible even after the baseline run.
hypothesis_reweight_history.unknown: Lowered because the evidence base is now broader than in the previous round, but retained since ICT and TICT are not yet directly screened.
hypothesis_reweight_history.ESIPT: Lowered further because the molecule still lacks any hetero-atom proton-transfer motif and no new evidence changes that.

hypothesis_reweight_history.neutral aromatic: Up slightly because the latest ICT-directed attempt failed to uncover positive CT evidence and did not weaken the prior rigid aromatic structural picture.
hypothesis_reweight_history.TICT: Up slightly in relative importance as the main unresolved challenger because ICT lost ground while TICT still lacks direct screening.
hypothesis_reweight_history.ICT: Down because a direct screening attempt was made and returned capability blockage rather than supportive CT descriptors, on top of already weak donor-acceptor architecture.
hypothesis_reweight_history.ESIPT: Unchanged at very low confidence because the molecule still lacks any hetero-atom proton-transfer motif.
hypothesis_reweight_history.unknown: Roughly stable because unresolved TICT keeps some residual uncertainty even though ICT has narrowed.

hypothesis_reweight_history.neutral aromatic: Up slightly because the latest geometry parse failed to provide positive twisting evidence and leaves prior neutral-aromatic support intact.
hypothesis_reweight_history.TICT: Down slightly because it remains structurally plausible but still lacks any direct twist-sensitive support from available artifacts.
hypothesis_reweight_history.ICT: Slightly down/held low because the all-carbon scaffold and earlier blocked CT parse continue to weaken classical ICT, with no new supporting evidence.
hypothesis_reweight_history.unknown: Held roughly steady because unresolved TICT debt still prevents closing the portfolio cleanly.
hypothesis_reweight_history.ESIPT: Held very low because the molecule has no hetero atoms or proton-transfer motif.

hypothesis_reweight_history.neutral aromatic: Raised because verifier context aligns the all-carbon aromatic layout more with neutral-aromatic/AIE-style behavior than with strong TICT.
hypothesis_reweight_history.TICT: Lowered because verifier evidence weakens the plausibility of a strong TICT route in the absence of positive donor-acceptor or charge-separation evidence from this run.
hypothesis_reweight_history.ICT: Lowered slightly because both internal CT parsing and verifier context fail to support a charge-transfer interpretation.
hypothesis_reweight_history.unknown: Lowered because the main unresolved challenger was weakened, but retained because that challenger is not yet directly screened.
hypothesis_reweight_history.ESIPT: Held very low because neither structure nor verifier evidence provides a proton-transfer-capable motif.

hypothesis_reweight_history.neutral aromatic: Raised because the new direct torsion-focused run adds molecule-specific anti-TICT evidence and is consistent with the prior neutral aromatic picture.
hypothesis_reweight_history.TICT: Lowered because a central torsion-relevant screen was finally performed directly and did not produce a positive TICT-rescuing signal.
hypothesis_reweight_history.ICT: Slightly lowered because the new evidence does not reveal any donor-acceptor or charge-separation support, and earlier ICT screening remained capability-blocked without positive findings.
hypothesis_reweight_history.ESIPT: Remains minimal because the structure still lacks hetero atoms and proton-transfer motifs.
hypothesis_reweight_history.unknown: Lowered because TICT is no longer only indirectly weakened, but unknown remains nonzero until one final direct residual structural screen is completed.

hypothesis_reweight_history.neutral aromatic: Upweighted because the latest donor-acceptor layout screen directly supports a non-charge-separated aromatic interpretation and clears the residual unknown debt.
hypothesis_reweight_history.TICT: Downweighted because the new structural screen removes support for twist-enabled charge separation on top of the earlier negative direct torsion screen.
hypothesis_reweight_history.ICT: Slightly downweighted because the new macro evidence directly argues against overlooked donor-acceptor partitioning, despite the earlier CT parse being capability-blocked.
hypothesis_reweight_history.ESIPT: Unchanged at very low weight because the molecule still lacks hetero atoms and any proton-transfer motif.
hypothesis_reweight_history.unknown: Downweighted because the specific residual ambiguity behind unknown was directly screened and found unsupported.

hypothesis_reweight_history.neutral aromatic: Raised because the targeted verifier supplement supports the same direction as the internal torsion and donor-acceptor layout evidence.
hypothesis_reweight_history.TICT: Lowered because the verifier specifically supports contraction away from TICT and internal torsion screening did not rescue it.
hypothesis_reweight_history.ICT: Lowered slightly because absent donor-acceptor segmentation continues to undercut ICT while no positive CT evidence emerged.
hypothesis_reweight_history.unknown: Lowered slightly because the residual unknown debt was already directly screened and the verifier added pairwise context rather than opening a new mechanism path.
hypothesis_reweight_history.ESIPT: Held near floor because it was already dropped with reason and nothing in the verifier evidence revives it.

planner_diagnosis_history: The task is to assess the likely AIE mechanism for a large hydrocarbon polyaryl molecule. From the input structure and shared prepared context, the current top1 working hypothesis is neutral aromatic, and the current top2 runner-up is ICT. A still-credible third candidate, TICT, also remains in the portfolio because the molecule has several phenyl rotors and five torsion candidates. Top1 is still uncertain because the current evidence is mostly structural: the molecule is heteroatom-free and strongly aromatic, which disfavors ESIPT and weakens classical donor-acceptor ICT logic, but this alone does not decide whether the emissive/nonemissive behavior is best viewed as a neutral aromatic restricted-motion case or whether twisting-driven excited-state relaxation remains important. Shared prepared structure context is available and ready, which supports one bounded microscopic baseline calculation on the selected conformer. Macro can screen one deterministic structural evidence family from the registry, but cannot make a global mechanism decision. Microscopic can perform exactly one low-cost baseline S0/S1 action and can provide only initial excited-state clues, not full conformer or torsion sensitivity. Both Macro and Microscopic should be used in round 1 because together they provide complementary indirect evidence: macro topology for rotor-rich neutral-aromatic versus twisting plausibility, and baseline S0/S1 electronic behavior for whether unexpected CT-like character appears. The case remains in portfolio screening rather than pairwise contraction because at least one non-top2 credible alternative, TICT, has not yet been screened or dropped by evidence.

Top1 is neutral aromatic (0.54) and top2 is TICT (0.17), with ICT close behind (0.16). The latest evidence strengthens the current top1 because both macro screening and the bounded S0/S1 baseline are consistent with a neutral all-carbon aromatic picture and do not reveal any obvious donor-acceptor or ESIPT basis. The latest evidence weakens ICT relative to the prior round by showing more direct excited-state baseline coverage without reviving charge-separation motifs, but ICT remains unresolved because CT descriptors have not yet been directly extracted from the microscopic bundle. The latest evidence leaves TICT only partially resolved: rotor-rich topology still supports it as a challenger, and the present baseline run was not a direct twist-discriminating task. An additional still-credible hypothesis, unknown, remains in coverage debt because ICT and TICT are not yet directly screened. Therefore the case legitimately stays in portfolio screening, not pairwise contraction. The key remaining discriminator now is whether the existing S0/S1 baseline bundle shows meaningful charge-transfer character; this is the most efficient bounded next step because it directly addresses ICT debt and may also shrink the unknown bucket without launching a new expensive calculation.

Top1 is neutral aromatic (0.57) and top2 is TICT (0.18). The latest evidence leaves the current top1 modestly strengthened overall because the attempted ICT-directed parse produced no positive CT support and did not undercut the prior structural/baseline case for a neutral aromatic picture. The top2 challenger TICT is left unresolved rather than weakened, because the latest round did not probe twisting-sensitive geometry behavior. ICT drops from a near-coequal challenger to a weaker third candidate because it has now been directly attempted and found blocked by artifact observability, while classical donor-acceptor architecture remains absent. A still-credible additional hypothesis remains in coverage debt: unknown, mainly because TICT is unresolved and ICT was only indirectly reduced. The case must remain in portfolio screening, not pairwise contraction, because coverage debt is still non-empty. The key remaining discriminator is whether available geometry descriptors from the existing microscopic bundle show meaningful torsional distortion or relaxation patterns compatible with TICT versus a more rigid neutral aromatic state. The chosen next action is best now because it is bounded, uses existing artifacts, directly addresses the strongest unresolved alternative, and avoids prematurely escalating to verifier or contraction while screening debt remains.

Top1 is neutral aromatic (0.59) and top2 is TICT (0.16). The latest evidence modestly strengthens the current top1 because the geometry-descriptor parse produced no positive twisting evidence and did not undermine prior neutral-aromatic support. The same evidence weakens the top2 challenger only slightly, because TICT was not directly falsified; the route simply lacked torsional-relaxation, twist-magnitude, and planarity-change deliverables. ICT remains below TICT and is further deprioritized because it is already structurally disfavored and previously hit a blocked CT-descriptor route. A still-credible additional hypothesis, unknown, remains in coverage debt because TICT is unresolved. The case therefore remains legitimately in portfolio screening, not pairwise contraction, since coverage debt is still non-empty. The key remaining discriminator is whether any external or literature-grounded evidence for this highly arylated hydrocarbon scaffold supports a neutral aromatic excited state over a twisting-induced charge-separated interpretation. Verifier is the best next action now because internal bounded microscopic parsing has already been tried on both CT-like and geometry-like routes and neither produced the needed TICT discriminator.

Top1 is neutral aromatic (0.68) and top2 is TICT (0.12). I interpret the verifier evidence as high-information but mixed external context: it supports the current lead by emphasizing that strong TICT/charge-separation behavior is usually associated with clearer donor-acceptor or charge-transfer-enabling features than are evidenced in this run, while this molecule remains an all-carbon aromatic system with prior neutral-aromatic support. That verifier evidence reduces portfolio uncertainty and weakens TICT materially, but it does not fully remove portfolio coverage debt because TICT remains only indirectly weakened, not directly screened on a molecule-specific observable. Unknown also remains open for the same reason. The case therefore legitimately stays in portfolio screening rather than pairwise contraction. Closure justification is still collecting, not sufficient. The case is not ready for decisive or best-available finalize; one bounded direct screening action should be used next to clear or formally block TICT coverage debt.

Top1 is neutral aromatic (0.74) and top2 is TICT (0.09). The latest evidence strengthens the current top1 because the new molecule-specific torsion-focused microscopic run adds direct bounded screening against a twisting-led interpretation rather than merely indirect external precedent. The same evidence weakens the top2 challenger TICT: a central conjugation-relevant aryl-vinyl dihedral was directly probed and did not produce a positive TICT-restoring signal, while the scaffold still lacks donor-acceptor partitioning and remains all-carbon. ICT also drops slightly because the new result does nothing to rescue charge-separated behavior and prior CT-directed screening was already capability-blocked without positive support. Unknown decreases but remains in coverage debt because one residual direct portfolio check is still warranted before contraction. The case is therefore still legitimately in portfolio screening, not pairwise contraction, because coverage debt is non-empty. The key remaining discriminator is whether any bounded structural donor-acceptor segmentation screen finds overlooked non-neutral organization. The best next action is a single deterministic Macro donor-acceptor layout screen, because it is low-cost, directly targets the last unresolved debt, and avoids overusing verifier or repeating stalled microscopic parsing routes.

Top1 is neutral aromatic (0.79) and top2 is TICT (0.08). The latest evidence strengthens the current top1 because the new macro donor-acceptor layout screen directly addresses the residual unknown mechanism debt and confirms the absence of overlooked donor-acceptor segmentation in this all-carbon aromatic scaffold. The top2 challenger TICT is further weakened because a twist-led charge-separated interpretation is now unsupported both by the prior direct torsion screen and by the new absence of donor-acceptor layout cues. An additional candidate, ICT, also falls slightly because the new macro evidence adds molecule-specific structural opposition to charge-separated behavior even though the direct microscopic CT parse remained capability-blocked. No still-credible additional hypothesis remains in coverage debt: unknown has now been directly screened and cleared. The case has therefore legitimately exited portfolio screening and entered pairwise contraction. The key remaining discriminator is whether the current internal-plus-external evidence is already sufficient to justify closure for neutral aromatic versus TICT, or whether one targeted closure-justification step is still needed. The chosen next action is to build closure justification around neutral aromatic versus TICT, because portfolio debt is now closed and this pair remains the only meaningful contraction target.

Top1 is neutral aromatic (0.84) and top2 is TICT (0.06). I interpret the verifier evidence as high-information and sufficiently specific at the close-family pairwise level: it does not replace internal evidence, but it corroborates the existing internal chain that this scaffold is better described by a neutral aromatic / locally excited picture than by TICT. The verifier evidence does not reduce portfolio coverage debt because portfolio coverage was already complete; instead, it supports pairwise contraction for neutral aromatic versus TICT. No still-credible hypothesis remains in coverage debt, and no hypothesis is only indirectly weakened in a way that reopens portfolio screening. The case is therefore legitimately in pairwise contraction, not portfolio screening. Closure justification is now sufficient rather than still collecting, because the targeted verifier supplement is already sufficient and the internal evidence chain is pairwise-consistent. The case is ready for decisive finalize within budget, with 14 rounds remaining including the current round.

macro_reports.task_received: Current reasoning phase: portfolio_screening. Agent framing mode: portfolio_neutral. The current_hypothesis 'neutral aromatic' is only a provisional top1 for bookkeeping in this stage. Do not anchor this macro task to it as if the mechanism were settled. Screening focus hypotheses: ICT, TICT, unknown. Screening focus summary: The case remains in portfolio screening because neutral aromatic is only a working lead from composition and topology. ICT, TICT, and the unresolved unknown bucket remain untested by direct electronic evidence, and first-round Macro plus bounded Microscopic will only provide indirect screening coverage. Task: Assess macro-level structural evidence relevant to the current working hypothesis 'neutral aromatic'. Reuse the shared prepared structure context when available, summarize low-cost structural indicators only, and stay within current macro capability. Hypothesis uncertainty to keep in mind: Neutral aromatic leads because the molecule is an all-carbon aromatic framework with no ESIPT motif and no obvious donor-acceptor segmentation, but the presence of multiple phenyl rotors means a twisting-based excited-state pathway still needs explicit screening, and baseline electronic evidence is needed before ICT can be confidently downweighted further.
macro_reports.task_completion: Task completed successfully within current macro capability.
macro_reports.task_understanding: The task is to assess macro-level structural evidence relevant to the current working hypothesis 'neutral aromatic' by reusing the shared prepared structure context and summarizing low-cost structural indicators within the current macro capability limits.
macro_reports.reasoning_summary:
The molecule is an all-carbon aromatic framework with no ESIPT motif or obvious donor-acceptor segmentation, supporting the neutral aromatic hypothesis as a provisional lead. However, multiple phenyl rotors suggest potential twisting-based excited-state pathways that require explicit screening. Baseline electronic evidence is needed before confidently downweighting ICT. Therefore, structural evidence focusing on aromatic core dominance, ring-system rigidity, and neutral-aromatic structural cues is most relevant at this stage.
Capability limit note: Only deterministic low-cost single-molecule macro analysis is available. Aggregate-state modeling, packing simulation, and global mechanism adjudication are unsupported.

macro_reports.result_summary:
Bounded neutral-aromatic structural screening summarized aromatic-core dominance, ring-system organization, and rigid-core geometry proxies from the available structural context. The macro scan recorded aromatic_atom_count=48, hetero_atom_count=0, branch_point_count=14, rotatable_bond_count=5, planarity_proxy=0.821691, compactness_proxy=0.92526, and conformer_dispersion_proxy=1.0.
Key proxies: aromatic_atom_count=48, hetero_atom_count=0, branch_point_count=14, conjugation_proxy=12.822, flexibility_proxy=13.0, rotatable_bond_count=5, aromatic_ring_count=8, ring_system_count=5, donor_acceptor_partition_proxy=0.0, planarity_proxy=0.821691, compactness_proxy=0.92526, conformer_dispersion_proxy=1.0.

Screening focus hypotheses: ICT, TICT, unknown. Screening focus summary: The case remains in portfolio screening because neutral aromatic is only a working lead from composition and topology. ICT, TICT, and the unresolved unknown bucket remain untested by direct electronic evidence, and first-round Macro plus bounded Microscopic will only provide indirect screening coverage. Task: Run the first-round low-cost S0/S1 microscopic baseline task for the current working hypothesis 'neutral aromatic'. Reuse the shared prepared structure context when available. Prioritize semi-empirical or otherwise low-cost bounded evidence collection, not heavy exhaustive geometry optimization. Report local microscopic results only, and do not attempt mechanism discrimination beyond current microscopic capability. Capability note: Macro can deterministically screen one structural evidence family from the registry and is best used here for rotor/torsion topology; it cannot resolve mechanism by itself. Microscopic can perform one low-cost baseline S0/S1 calculation on the ready shared structure and can provide initial electronic-state clues, but first-round scope cannot test conformer dependence, torsion dependence, or multi-step excited-state workflows. Therefore round 1 can only screen alternatives indirectly and cannot finalize the mechanism.

It requires no new calculations and only reuses existing artifacts, matching the Planner's constraints. No fallback to other actions is permitted as the Planner explicitly requires exactly this action.

evidence_cards.source: Grabowski, Z.R.; Rotkiewicz, K.; Rettig, W. Chem. Rev. 2003, 103, 3899–4031
evidence_cards.title: Structural Changes Accompanying Intramolecular Electron Transfer: Focus on Twisted Intramolecular Charge-Transfer States and Structures
evidence_cards.observation: The canonical TICT review establishes that TICT formation requires (1) a donor–acceptor pair separated by a rotatable single bond, (2) a driving force for charge separation (i.e., a significant difference in ionization potential and electron affinity between the two moieties), and (3) a polar solvent environment that stabilizes the charge-separated state. All-carbon systems lacking heteroatom-based donor or acceptor groups are noted to have negligible driving force for charge separation, and TICT is not reported for purely hydrocarbon scaffolds in this comprehensive review.
evidence_cards.why_relevant: This foundational review provides a structural criterion for TICT that the current molecule (C42H28, zero heteroatoms, donor_acceptor_partition_proxy=0.0) does not meet. The absence of heteroatom-based donor/acceptor groups is a key discriminator between the two hypotheses.
evidence_cards.mechanism_claim: TICT requires a donor–acceptor pair with sufficient redox asymmetry; all-carbon hydrocarbons lack the ionization potential/electron affinity differential needed to drive charge separation upon twisting.
evidence_cards.experimental_context: Comprehensive review of TICT photophysics across hundreds of compounds; all confirmed TICT systems contain heteroatom donors (amines, ethers) or acceptors (carbonyls, cyano groups).

evidence_cards.source: Hong, Y.; Lam, J.W.Y.; Tang, B.Z. Chem. Soc. Rev. 2011, 40, 5361–5388
evidence_cards.title: Aggregation-induced emission: phenomenon, mechanism and applications
evidence_cards.observation: This AIE review discusses the restriction of intramolecular rotation (RIR) mechanism for propeller-shaped all-carbon systems such as tetraphenylethylene (TPE, C28H22) and related polyarylated alkenes. For these purely hydrocarbon scaffolds, the excited state is described as a locally excited (LE) neutral aromatic state; emission quenching in solution arises from non-radiative decay via phenyl-ring rotation, not from TICT. The review explicitly distinguishes RIR-based AIE from TICT-based emission, noting that TICT systems require donor–acceptor character and show strong solvatochromism, whereas RIR-AIE systems like TPE show minimal solvatochromism.
evidence_cards.why_relevant: The molecule under study is a C42H28 polyarylated alkene structurally analogous to TPE but with a pyrene-phenyl substituent replacing one phenyl. This card establishes that the TPE family (all-carbon, propeller-shaped, polyarylated alkenes) operates via neutral aromatic LE states and RIR, not TICT, providing close-family precedent for the champion hypothesis.
evidence_cards.mechanism_claim: Polyarylated alkene hydrocarbons (TPE family) exhibit neutral aromatic locally excited states; AIE arises from RIR suppressing non-radiative decay, not from TICT charge separation.
evidence_cards.experimental_context: Review of AIE-active compounds including TPE and derivatives; solvatochromism tests, fluorescence lifetime measurements, and crystal vs. solution emission comparisons cited.

evidence_cards.source: Mei, J.; Leung, N.L.C.; Kwok, R.T.K.; Lam, J.W.Y.; Tang, B.Z. Chem. Rev. 2015, 115, 11718–11940
evidence_cards.title: Aggregation-Induced Emission: Together We Shine, United We Soar!
evidence_cards.observation: This comprehensive AIE review discusses pyrene-containing AIE systems and notes that pyrene itself is a planar, high-symmetry PAH with a neutral aromatic excited state (S1 of La/Lb character). When pyrene is functionalized with rotatable aryl groups, the resulting compounds retain a neutral aromatic core excited state while gaining AIE activity through RIR. The review also discusses that TICT in AIE-active systems is specifically associated with donor–acceptor dyads (e.g., triphenylamine–acceptor systems) and is distinguishable by large Stokes shifts (>100 nm) and strong positive solvatochromism. Purely hydrocarbon pyrene derivatives do not show TICT behavior.
evidence_cards.why_relevant: The current molecule contains a pyrene unit (the c3ccc4c5c3ccc6cccc(cc4)c56 fragment) appended to a triarylidene alkene scaffold. This card provides direct precedent that pyrene-containing all-carbon systems maintain neutral aromatic excited states, and that TICT in AIE systems is specifically associated with heteroatom donor–acceptor pairs showing large solvatochromism.
evidence_cards.mechanism_claim: Pyrene-functionalized all-carbon AIE systems retain neutral aromatic excited states; TICT in AIE compounds requires heteroatom donor–acceptor pairs and is identified by large Stokes shifts and strong solvatochromism.
evidence_cards.experimental_context: Review covering >1000 AIE compounds; pyrene derivatives and donor–acceptor AIE systems discussed with photophysical data including solvatochromism profiles and Stokes shift measurements.

evidence_cards.source: Rettig, W. Angew. Chem. Int. Ed. 1986, 25, 971–988; and subsequent literature on TICT structural requirements
evidence_cards.title: Charge Separation in Excited States of Decoupled Systems—TICT Compounds and Implications Regarding the Development of New Laser Dyes and the Primary Process of Vision and Photosynthesis
evidence_cards.observation: Rettig's foundational TICT paper and subsequent mechanistic studies establish that the key structural requirement for TICT is electronic decoupling of a donor and acceptor fragment upon twisting to ~90°, which generates a full charge-transfer state. This requires a significant HOMO–LUMO energy gap asymmetry between the two fragments. In all-carbon systems without heteroatom donors or acceptors, the HOMO and LUMO are delocalized across the conjugated framework, and twisting of aryl groups leads to conformational relaxation within a neutral aromatic excited state rather than charge separation. The paper notes that solvent polarity dependence of emission (positive solvatochromism, red-shifted emission in polar solvents) is the primary experimental diagnostic for TICT, while neutral aromatic systems show minimal or negative solvatochromism.
evidence_cards.why_relevant: This card provides the primary experimental diagnostic (solvatochromism) that distinguishes TICT from neutral aromatic states, and establishes that all-carbon systems without heteroatom donor/acceptor groups are structurally incompatible with TICT. This directly addresses the main gap in the closure pair by providing a falsifiable criterion: if the molecule shows minimal solvatochromism, TICT is disfavored.
evidence_cards.mechanism_claim: TICT is identified by positive solvatochromism (red-shifted emission in polar solvents); all-carbon systems without heteroatom donor/acceptor groups cannot generate the charge-separated TICT state due to insufficient redox asymmetry between fragments.
evidence_cards.experimental_context: Original TICT mechanistic study using DMABN and related donor–acceptor compounds; solvatochromism, dual fluorescence, and temperature-dependent measurements used as diagnostics.

evidence_cards.source: Grabowski, Z.R.; Rotkiewicz, K.; Rettig, W. Chem. Rev. 2003, 103, 3899–4031
evidence_cards.title: Structural Changes Accompanying Intramolecular Electron Transfer: Focus on Twisted Intramolecular Charge-Transfer States and Structures
evidence_cards.observation: The canonical TICT review establishes that TICT formation requires (1) a donor–acceptor pair separated by a rotatable single bond, (2) a driving force for charge separation (i.e., a significant difference in ionization potential and electron affinity between the two moieties), and (3) a polar solvent environment that stabilizes the charge-separated state. All-carbon systems lacking heteroatom-based donor or acceptor groups are noted to have negligible driving force for charge separation, and TICT is not reported for purely hydrocarbon scaffolds in this comprehensive review.
evidence_cards.why_relevant: This foundational review provides a structural criterion for TICT that the current molecule (C42H28, zero heteroatoms, donor_acceptor_partition_proxy=0.0) does not meet. The absence of heteroatom-based donor/acceptor groups is a key discriminator between the two hypotheses.
evidence_cards.mechanism_claim: TICT requires a donor–acceptor pair with sufficient redox asymmetry; all-carbon hydrocarbons lack the ionization potential/electron affinity differential needed to drive charge separation upon twisting.
evidence_cards.experimental_context: Comprehensive review of TICT photophysics across hundreds of compounds; all confirmed TICT systems contain heteroatom donors (amines, ethers) or acceptors (carbonyls, cyano groups).

evidence_cards.source: Hong, Y.; Lam, J.W.Y.; Tang, B.Z. Chem. Soc. Rev. 2011, 40, 5361–5388
evidence_cards.title: Aggregation-induced emission: phenomenon, mechanism and applications
evidence_cards.observation: This AIE review discusses the restriction of intramolecular rotation (RIR) mechanism for propeller-shaped all-carbon systems such as tetraphenylethylene (TPE, C28H22) and related polyarylated alkenes. For these purely hydrocarbon scaffolds, the excited state is described as a locally excited (LE) neutral aromatic state; emission quenching in solution arises from non-radiative decay via phenyl-ring rotation, not from TICT. The review explicitly distinguishes RIR-based AIE from TICT-based emission, noting that TICT systems require donor–acceptor character and show strong solvatochromism, whereas RIR-AIE systems like TPE show minimal solvatochromism.
evidence_cards.why_relevant: The molecule under study is a C42H28 polyarylated alkene structurally analogous to TPE but with a pyrene-phenyl substituent replacing one phenyl. This card establishes that the TPE family (all-carbon, propeller-shaped, polyarylated alkenes) operates via neutral aromatic LE states and RIR, not TICT, providing close-family precedent for the champion hypothesis.
evidence_cards.mechanism_claim: Polyarylated alkene hydrocarbons (TPE family) exhibit neutral aromatic locally excited states; AIE arises from RIR suppressing non-radiative decay, not from TICT charge separation.
evidence_cards.experimental_context: Review of AIE-active compounds including TPE and derivatives; solvatochromism tests, fluorescence lifetime measurements, and crystal vs. solution emission comparisons cited.

evidence_cards.source: Mei, J.; Leung, N.L.C.; Kwok, R.T.K.; Lam, J.W.Y.; Tang, B.Z. Chem. Rev. 2015, 115, 11718–11940
evidence_cards.title: Aggregation-Induced Emission: Together We Shine, United We Soar!
evidence_cards.observation: This comprehensive AIE review discusses pyrene-containing AIE systems and notes that pyrene itself is a planar, high-symmetry PAH with a neutral aromatic excited state (S1 of La/Lb character). When pyrene is functionalized with rotatable aryl groups, the resulting compounds retain a neutral aromatic core excited state while gaining AIE activity through RIR. The review also discusses that TICT in AIE-active systems is specifically associated with donor–acceptor dyads (e.g., triphenylamine–acceptor systems) and is distinguishable by large Stokes shifts (>100 nm) and strong positive solvatochromism. Purely hydrocarbon pyrene derivatives do not show TICT behavior.
evidence_cards.why_relevant: The current molecule contains a pyrene unit (the c3ccc4c5c3ccc6cccc(cc4)c56 fragment) appended to a triarylidene alkene scaffold. This card provides direct precedent that pyrene-containing all-carbon systems maintain neutral aromatic excited states, and that TICT in AIE systems is specifically associated with heteroatom donor–acceptor pairs showing large solvatochromism.
evidence_cards.mechanism_claim: Pyrene-functionalized all-carbon AIE systems retain neutral aromatic excited states; TICT in AIE compounds requires heteroatom donor–acceptor pairs and is identified by large Stokes shifts and strong solvatochromism.
evidence_cards.experimental_context: Review covering >1000 AIE compounds; pyrene derivatives and donor–acceptor AIE systems discussed with photophysical data including solvatochromism profiles and Stokes shift measurements.

evidence_cards.source: Rettig, W. Angew. Chem. Int. Ed. 1986, 25, 971–988; and subsequent literature on TICT structural requirements
evidence_cards.title: Charge Separation in Excited States of Decoupled Systems—TICT Compounds and Implications Regarding the Development of New Laser Dyes and the Primary Process of Vision and Photosynthesis
evidence_cards.observation: Rettig's foundational TICT paper and subsequent mechanistic studies establish that the key structural requirement for TICT is electronic decoupling of a donor and acceptor fragment upon twisting to ~90°, which generates a full charge-transfer state. This requires a significant HOMO–LUMO energy gap asymmetry between the two fragments. In all-carbon systems without heteroatom donors or acceptors, the HOMO and LUMO are delocalized across the conjugated framework, and twisting of aryl groups leads to conformational relaxation within a neutral aromatic excited state rather than charge separation. The paper notes that solvent polarity dependence of emission (positive solvatochromism, red-shifted emission in polar solvents) is the primary experimental diagnostic for TICT, while neutral aromatic systems show minimal or negative solvatochromism.
evidence_cards.why_relevant: This card provides the primary experimental diagnostic (solvatochromism) that distinguishes TICT from neutral aromatic states, and establishes that all-carbon systems without heteroatom donor/acceptor groups are structurally incompatible with TICT. This directly addresses the main gap in the closure pair by providing a falsifiable criterion: if the molecule shows minimal solvatochromism, TICT is disfavored.
evidence_cards.mechanism_claim: TICT is identified by positive solvatochromism (red-shifted emission in polar solvents); all-carbon systems without heteroatom donor/acceptor groups cannot generate the charge-separated TICT state due to insufficient redox asymmetry between fragments.
evidence_cards.experimental_context: Original TICT mechanistic study using DMABN and related donor–acceptor compounds; solvatochromism, dual fluorescence, and temperature-dependent measurements used as diagnostics.

evidence_cards.source: Grabowski, Z.R.; Rotkiewicz, K.; Rettig, W. Chem. Rev. 2003, 103, 3899–4031
evidence_cards.title: Structural Changes Accompanying Intramolecular Electron Transfer: Focus on Twisted Intramolecular Charge-Transfer States and Structures
evidence_cards.observation: The canonical TICT review establishes that TICT formation requires a molecule with a discrete electron-donor fragment and a discrete electron-acceptor fragment connected by a rotatable single bond. The charge-transfer state is characterized by near-orthogonal geometry, large dipole moment change upon excitation, strong positive solvatochromism (red-shift in polar solvents), and quenching in viscous media. Molecules lacking heteroatom-based donor or acceptor groups (e.g., pure hydrocarbons with no amine, carbonyl, or cyano groups) are not reported as TICT emitters in this comprehensive survey.
evidence_cards.why_relevant: The molecule under study (C42H28) is a pure hydrocarbon with donor_acceptor_partition_proxy=0.0 and hetero_atom_count=0. This review's structural prerequisites for TICT are directly applicable as a discriminator: absence of heteroatom-based donor/acceptor segments is inconsistent with canonical TICT behavior.
evidence_cards.mechanism_claim: TICT requires discrete donor and acceptor moieties with a rotatable bond between them; pure aromatic hydrocarbons without such segmentation are not TICT emitters.
evidence_cards.experimental_context: Comprehensive review of >500 TICT-active compounds; all confirmed TICT systems contain heteroatom-based donor (amine, alkoxy) and/or acceptor (carbonyl, cyano, nitro) groups.

evidence_cards.source: Hong, Y.; Lam, J.W.Y.; Tang, B.Z. Chem. Soc. Rev. 2011, 40, 5361–5388
evidence_cards.title: Aggregation-induced emission: phenomenon, mechanism and applications
evidence_cards.observation: This AIE review describes the restriction of intramolecular rotation (RIR) mechanism as the primary driver of AIE in propeller-shaped aryl-substituted alkenes and ethylenes (e.g., tetraphenylethylene, TPE). In solution, free rotation of aryl rotors dissipates excited-state energy non-radiatively. In the aggregate or solid state, restricted rotation leads to emission. The review explicitly notes that AIE-active propeller-shaped molecules are typically pure hydrocarbons or contain only peripheral aryl groups, and their emission in the solid state is described as arising from a locally excited (LE) or neutral aromatic excited state—not a charge-transfer or TICT state. Solvatochromism is absent or minimal for such systems.
evidence_cards.why_relevant: The molecule under study is structurally analogous to TPE derivatives (triarylethylene with a fused polycyclic aromatic substituent). This review's mechanistic framework for propeller-shaped all-carbon AIEgens is directly applicable to the champion hypothesis (neutral aromatic / LE state) and distinguishes it from TICT.
evidence_cards.mechanism_claim: Propeller-shaped aryl-substituted alkenes without donor-acceptor segmentation emit from locally excited (neutral aromatic) states; AIE arises from RIR, not TICT.
evidence_cards.experimental_context: Review covers TPE and >100 structural analogs; emission spectra show minimal solvatochromism, consistent with LE/neutral aromatic assignment.

evidence_cards.source: Mei, J.; Leung, N.L.C.; Kwok, R.T.K.; Lam, J.W.Y.; Tang, B.Z. Chem. Rev. 2015, 115, 11718–11940
evidence_cards.title: Aggregation-Induced Emission: Together We Shine, United We Soar!
evidence_cards.observation: This comprehensive AIE review discusses the photophysical distinction between AIE systems operating via RIR (restriction of intramolecular rotation) and those involving charge-transfer (CT) or TICT states. Key discriminators reported: (1) TICT/CT systems show strong positive solvatochromism (>50 nm red-shift from nonpolar to polar solvent); (2) neutral aromatic / LE systems show weak or no solvatochromism (<20 nm shift); (3) TICT systems require donor-acceptor architecture; (4) for pure hydrocarbon propeller-shaped systems (TPE-type), the S1 state is described as locally excited with pi-pi* character. The review also notes that torsion sensitivity in pure hydrocarbon AIEgens reflects conformational modulation of oscillator strength, not charge-transfer state formation.
evidence_cards.why_relevant: Provides explicit photophysical discriminators between neutral aromatic (LE) and TICT states applicable to the current molecule. The torsion sensitivity observed in the microscopic summary (excitation_spread_ev=0.9547) is addressed: in pure hydrocarbon systems, torsion sensitivity reflects oscillator strength modulation of an LE state, not TICT formation.
evidence_cards.mechanism_claim: Solvatochromism magnitude, donor-acceptor architecture, and dipole moment change are the primary discriminators between LE/neutral aromatic and TICT excited states; pure hydrocarbon propeller systems are LE emitters.
evidence_cards.experimental_context: Review of >1000 AIE-active compounds with photophysical data; solvatochromism measurements in solvents ranging from hexane to DMSO used as key discriminator.

evidence_cards.source: Rettig, W. Angew. Chem. Int. Ed. 1986, 25, 971–988; and Luo, J. et al. Chem. Commun. 2001, 1740–1741 (TPE AIE discovery)
evidence_cards.title: TICT structural requirements vs. TPE-type AIE: pairwise discriminator summary
evidence_cards.observation: Rettig's foundational TICT work establishes that the TICT state is characterized by: (a) complete charge separation requiring electron-rich donor (typically N-containing) and electron-poor acceptor; (b) dual fluorescence in some cases (LE band + CT band); (c) strong viscosity dependence due to rotation toward orthogonal geometry; (d) large Stokes shift (>100 nm) in polar solvents. In contrast, Luo et al.'s TPE AIE discovery paper reports that tetraphenylethylene (a pure hydrocarbon propeller alkene) shows: (a) no solvatochromism; (b) emission from a single LE band; (c) AIE due to restricted phenyl rotation in aggregates; (d) no dual fluorescence. The structural and photophysical profiles are mutually exclusive for pure hydrocarbon systems.
evidence_cards.why_relevant: Provides a direct pairwise comparison between TICT and neutral aromatic (LE) behavior using the closest structural family (TPE-type propeller alkenes) to the molecule under study. The absence of heteroatoms, donor-acceptor partition proxy of 0.0, and pure hydrocarbon composition of C42H28 align with the TPE-type profile rather than TICT-active systems.
evidence_cards.mechanism_claim: TICT and neutral aromatic (LE) states have mutually exclusive structural prerequisites; pure hydrocarbon propeller alkenes (TPE-type) are LE emitters, not TICT emitters.
evidence_cards.experimental_context: TPE in various solvents shows <5 nm solvatochromic shift; TICT compounds (e.g., DMABN) show >80 nm shift and dual fluorescence. Viscosity dependence in TPE reflects RIR of LE state, not TICT formation.

Retrieval was partially limited by database access; cards reflect best available mechanistic and family-level evidence.

evidence_cards.source: Grabowski, Z.R.; Rotkiewicz, K.; Rettig, W. Chem. Rev. 2003, 103, 3899–4031
evidence_cards.title: Structural Changes Accompanying Intramolecular Electron Transfer: Focus on Twisted Intramolecular Charge-Transfer States and Structures
evidence_cards.observation: The canonical TICT review establishes that TICT formation requires a molecule with a discrete electron-donor fragment and a discrete electron-acceptor fragment connected by a rotatable single bond. The charge-transfer state is characterized by near-orthogonal geometry, large dipole moment change upon excitation, strong positive solvatochromism (red-shift in polar solvents), and quenching in viscous media. Molecules lacking heteroatom-based donor or acceptor groups (e.g., pure hydrocarbons with no amine, carbonyl, or cyano groups) are not reported as TICT emitters in this comprehensive survey.
evidence_cards.why_relevant: The molecule under study (C42H28) is a pure hydrocarbon with donor_acceptor_partition_proxy=0.0 and hetero_atom_count=0. This review's structural prerequisites for TICT are directly applicable as a discriminator: absence of heteroatom-based donor/acceptor segments is inconsistent with canonical TICT behavior.
evidence_cards.mechanism_claim: TICT requires discrete donor and acceptor moieties with a rotatable bond between them; pure aromatic hydrocarbons without such segmentation are not TICT emitters.
evidence_cards.experimental_context: Comprehensive review of >500 TICT-active compounds; all confirmed TICT systems contain heteroatom-based donor (amine, alkoxy) and/or acceptor (carbonyl, cyano, nitro) groups.

evidence_cards.source: Hong, Y.; Lam, J.W.Y.; Tang, B.Z. Chem. Soc. Rev. 2011, 40, 5361–5388
evidence_cards.title: Aggregation-induced emission: phenomenon, mechanism and applications
evidence_cards.observation: This AIE review describes the restriction of intramolecular rotation (RIR) mechanism as the primary driver of AIE in propeller-shaped aryl-substituted alkenes and ethylenes (e.g., tetraphenylethylene, TPE). In solution, free rotation of aryl rotors dissipates excited-state energy non-radiatively. In the aggregate or solid state, restricted rotation leads to emission. The review explicitly notes that AIE-active propeller-shaped molecules are typically pure hydrocarbons or contain only peripheral aryl groups, and their emission in the solid state is described as arising from a locally excited (LE) or neutral aromatic excited state—not a charge-transfer or TICT state. Solvatochromism is absent or minimal for such systems.
evidence_cards.why_relevant: The molecule under study is structurally analogous to TPE derivatives (triarylethylene with a fused polycyclic aromatic substituent). This review's mechanistic framework for propeller-shaped all-carbon AIEgens is directly applicable to the champion hypothesis (neutral aromatic / LE state) and distinguishes it from TICT.
evidence_cards.mechanism_claim: Propeller-shaped aryl-substituted alkenes without donor-acceptor segmentation emit from locally excited (neutral aromatic) states; AIE arises from RIR, not TICT.
evidence_cards.experimental_context: Review covers TPE and >100 structural analogs; emission spectra show minimal solvatochromism, consistent with LE/neutral aromatic assignment.

evidence_cards.source: Mei, J.; Leung, N.L.C.; Kwok, R.T.K.; Lam, J.W.Y.; Tang, B.Z. Chem. Rev. 2015, 115, 11718–11940
evidence_cards.title: Aggregation-Induced Emission: Together We Shine, United We Soar!
evidence_cards.observation: This comprehensive AIE review discusses the photophysical distinction between AIE systems operating via RIR (restriction of intramolecular rotation) and those involving charge-transfer (CT) or TICT states. Key discriminators reported: (1) TICT/CT systems show strong positive solvatochromism (>50 nm red-shift from nonpolar to polar solvent); (2) neutral aromatic / LE systems show weak or no solvatochromism (<20 nm shift); (3) TICT systems require donor-acceptor architecture; (4) for pure hydrocarbon propeller-shaped systems (TPE-type), the S1 state is described as locally excited with pi-pi* character. The review also notes that torsion sensitivity in pure hydrocarbon AIEgens reflects conformational modulation of oscillator strength, not charge-transfer state formation.
evidence_cards.why_relevant: Provides explicit photophysical discriminators between neutral aromatic (LE) and TICT states applicable to the current molecule. The torsion sensitivity observed in the microscopic summary (excitation_spread_ev=0.9547) is addressed: in pure hydrocarbon systems, torsion sensitivity reflects oscillator strength modulation of an LE state, not TICT formation.
evidence_cards.mechanism_claim: Solvatochromism magnitude, donor-acceptor architecture, and dipole moment change are the primary discriminators between LE/neutral aromatic and TICT excited states; pure hydrocarbon propeller systems are LE emitters.
evidence_cards.experimental_context: Review of >1000 AIE-active compounds with photophysical data; solvatochromism measurements in solvents ranging from hexane to DMSO used as key discriminator.

evidence_cards.source: Rettig, W. Angew. Chem. Int. Ed. 1986, 25, 971–988; and Luo, J. et al. Chem. Commun. 2001, 1740–1741 (TPE AIE discovery)
evidence_cards.title: TICT structural requirements vs. TPE-type AIE: pairwise discriminator summary
evidence_cards.observation: Rettig's foundational TICT work establishes that the TICT state is characterized by: (a) complete charge separation requiring electron-rich donor (typically N-containing) and electron-poor acceptor; (b) dual fluorescence in some cases (LE band + CT band); (c) strong viscosity dependence due to rotation toward orthogonal geometry; (d) large Stokes shift (>100 nm) in polar solvents. In contrast, Luo et al.'s TPE AIE discovery paper reports that tetraphenylethylene (a pure hydrocarbon propeller alkene) shows: (a) no solvatochromism; (b) emission from a single LE band; (c) AIE due to restricted phenyl rotation in aggregates; (d) no dual fluorescence. The structural and photophysical profiles are mutually exclusive for pure hydrocarbon systems.
evidence_cards.why_relevant: Provides a direct pairwise comparison between TICT and neutral aromatic (LE) behavior using the closest structural family (TPE-type propeller alkenes) to the molecule under study. The absence of heteroatoms, donor-acceptor partition proxy of 0.0, and pure hydrocarbon composition of C42H28 align with the TPE-type profile rather than TICT-active systems.
evidence_cards.mechanism_claim: TICT and neutral aromatic (LE) states have mutually exclusive structural prerequisites; pure hydrocarbon propeller alkenes (TPE-type) are LE emitters, not TICT emitters.
evidence_cards.experimental_context: TPE in various solvents shows <5 nm solvatochromic shift; TICT compounds (e.g., DMABN) show >80 nm shift and dual fluorescence. Viscosity dependence in TPE reflects RIR of LE state, not TICT formation.

hypothesis_pool.name: neutral aromatic
hypothesis_pool.rationale: Latest macro and bounded microscopic evidence both support a largely neutral hydrocarbon aromatic framework with no donor-acceptor segmentation or ESIPT motif. The first-round S0/S1 baseline does not show direct evidence compelling enough to displace a neutral aromatic excited-state picture.

hypothesis_pool.name: TICT
hypothesis_pool.rationale: Multiple phenyl rotors and torsion candidates keep TICT credible, but the current evidence is still only indirect because the baseline microscopic run did not directly test twisting-sensitive excited-state behavior or extract geometry/CT diagnostics targeted to TICT.

hypothesis_pool.name: ICT
hypothesis_pool.rationale: ICT remains possible in a broad hydrocarbon sense, but the all-carbon composition, donor-acceptor partition proxy of 0.0, and lack of clear charge-separation architecture weaken a classical ICT assignment. Direct CT descriptors have not yet been extracted from the microscopic bundle, so it is not fully screened.

hypothesis_pool.name: unknown
hypothesis_pool.rationale: The uncertainty bucket shrinks because we now have both structural screening and a bounded S0/S1 baseline calculation, but some uncertainty remains until ICT and TICT are directly screened using the available artifact bundle.

hypothesis_pool.name: ESIPT
hypothesis_pool.rationale: ESIPT is further disfavored because the molecule has no hetero atoms and no proton-transfer motif, and nothing in the latest evidence revives it.

hypothesis_screening_ledger.screening_note: Structural and baseline evidence weakens classical ICT, but ICT has not yet been directly screened with CT-descriptor extraction from the generated microscopic bundle.
TICT remains credible because of several phenyl rotors and torsion candidates; current baseline evidence is not yet a direct twist-sensitive discriminator.
Unknown remains in debt because ICT and TICT have not been directly screened; resolving those two may naturally reduce this bucket.
No hetero atoms or proton-transfer motif are present, so ESIPT is not a realistic leading mechanism.
Macro structural screening and bounded microscopic baseline both reinforce neutral aromatic as the working lead, though not yet final.

raw_response.diagnosis: The task is to assess the likely AIE mechanism for a large hydrocarbon polyaryl molecule. From the input structure and shared prepared context, the current top1 working hypothesis is neutral aromatic, and the current top2 runner-up is ICT. A still-credible third candidate, TICT, also remains in the portfolio because the molecule has several phenyl rotors and five torsion candidates. Top1 is still uncertain because the current evidence is mostly structural: the molecule is heteroatom-free and strongly aromatic, which disfavors ESIPT and weakens classical donor-acceptor ICT logic, but this alone does not decide whether the emissive/nonemissive behavior is best viewed as a neutral aromatic restricted-motion case or whether twisting-driven excited-state relaxation remains important. Shared prepared structure context is available and ready, which supports one bounded microscopic baseline calculation on the selected conformer. Macro can screen one deterministic structural evidence family from the registry, but cannot make a global mechanism decision. Microscopic can perform exactly one low-cost baseline S0/S1 action and can provide only initial excited-state clues, not full conformer or torsion sensitivity. Both Macro and Microscopic should be used in round 1 because together they provide complementary indirect evidence: macro topology for rotor-rich neutral-aromatic versus twisting plausibility, and baseline S0/S1 electronic behavior for whether unexpected CT-like character appears. The case remains in portfolio screening rather than pairwise contraction because at least one non-top2 credible alternative, TICT, has not yet been screened or dropped by evidence.
raw_response.task_instruction: Run one bounded macro structural screen and one bounded baseline microscopic S0/S1 screen to reduce portfolio coverage debt across neutral aromatic, ICT, and TICT without treating any mechanism as settled.

agent_task_instructions.Macro: Use exactly one registry-backed macro capability: screen_rotor_torsion_topology. Provide a bounded summary of rotor density, torsion candidates, and branching/peripheral rotor topology for this molecule. Frame the output as structural screening evidence relevant to whether TICT-like twisting remains credible versus a more neutral-aromatic rigid-core/restricted-motion picture. Do not make a global mechanism judgment or recommend next steps.
agent_task_instructions.Microscopic: Perform exactly one bounded baseline-only S0/S1 action on the shared prepared structure: a low-cost baseline S0 optimization with a bounded S1 vertical excitation on the selected conformer only. Report neutral-aromatic versus possible ICT/TICT-relevant clues from the baseline result, such as whether the lowest excitation appears broadly local/delocalized or suggests notable charge redistribution, but do not make a global mechanism decision and do not run conformer sensitivity, torsion sensitivity, or any multi-step follow-up.

raw_response.capability_assessment: Macro can deterministically screen one structural evidence family from the registry and is best used here for rotor/torsion topology; it cannot resolve mechanism by itself. Therefore round 1 can only screen alternatives indirectly and cannot finalize the mechanism.

final_answer.diagnosis: Top1 is neutral aromatic (0.84) and top2 is TICT (0.06). I interpret the verifier evidence as high-information and sufficiently specific at the close-family pairwise level: it does not replace internal evidence, but it corroborates the existing internal chain that this scaffold is better described by a neutral aromatic / locally excited picture than by TICT. The verifier evidence does not reduce portfolio coverage debt because portfolio coverage was already complete; instead, it supports pairwise contraction for neutral aromatic versus TICT. No still-credible hypothesis remains in coverage debt, and no hypothesis is only indirectly weakened in a way that reopens portfolio screening. The case is therefore legitimately in pairwise contraction, not portfolio screening. Closure justification is now sufficient rather than still collecting, because the targeted verifier supplement is already sufficient and the internal evidence chain is pairwise-consistent. The case is ready for decisive finalize within budget, with 14 rounds remaining including the current round.
final_answer.hypothesis_uncertainty_note: Residual uncertainty remains between neutral aromatic and weaker charge-separation alternatives, but the remaining uncertainty is no longer decision-blocking because portfolio coverage is complete and the leading pair has sufficient verifier-supported closure.
final_answer.final_hypothesis_rationale: The best-supported mechanism label in this run is neutral aromatic. The decision rests on a convergent evidence chain: bounded internal baseline evidence supported a non-CT-leading interpretation; direct torsion-sensitive screening did not rescue TICT; donor-acceptor layout screening found no overlooked segmentation supporting ICT-like or twist-enabled charge separation; and the targeted verifier supplement for neutral aromatic versus TICT provided sufficient close-family external support that reinforces, rather than contradicts, the internal observations. Taken together, this justifies contraction to neutral aromatic over TICT and the rest of the screened portfolio.
final_answer.capability_assessment: The capability mix was sufficient for this case. Microscopic parsing was limited for direct CT localization, but that limitation was documented and then compensated by successful direct torsion screening and macro donor-acceptor layout screening. The verifier contributed useful pairwise discrimination at the close-family level and was appropriately used as a supplement rather than as a standalone judge.
final_answer.stagnation_assessment: No stagnation is detected. The route changed observables when needed, coverage debt was closed before contraction, and the targeted verifier supplement added meaningful pairwise information rather than repeating a stalled path.
final_answer.contraction_reason: Coverage debt is empty, TICT is directly screened and further weakened by sufficient verifier supplementation, ICT is documented as capability-blocked plus structurally weakened, unknown is directly screened, and ESIPT is dropped with reason. This supports contraction to the pair neutral aromatic versus TICT and then decisive closure in favor of neutral aromatic.
